coredata.dc:title: Facile hydrothermal synthesis of urchin-like NiCo2O4 spheres as efficient electrocatalysts for oxygen reduction reaction
coredata.prism:copyright: Crown copyright © 2013 Published by Elsevier Ltd. All rights reserved.

Xu, Qi-Zhi
Wang, Ji-Yu
Li, Nan
Guo, Shi-Heng
Su, Yu-Zhi
Wang, Hong-Juan
Zhang, Jian-Hua
Chen, Shuang
coredata.dc:description: In this work, we have successfully prepared urchin-like NiCo2O4 spheres via a facile hydrothermal method without any templates or surfactants. The as-prepared urchin-like NiCo2O4 spheres have uniform diameters of 6 μm with numerous small nanorods radially-grown from the center. Typical nanorods have diameters of 20–50 nm and lengths of 2–3 μm. Remarkably, urchin-like NiCo2O4 nanostructure as an alternative non-precious metal electrocatalyst for ORR exhibits catalytic performance and excellent cycling stability in alkaline environment.
coredata.dcterms:subject: NiCo2O4
Spheres
Electrocatalysts
Oxygen reduction reaction

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) XRD pattern, (b–d) SEM images of urchin-like NiCo2O4 spheres obtained for hydrothermal reaction 3 h at 180 °C.
(a–e) TEM, HRTEM images, SAED pattern of urchin-like NiCo2O4 spheres, and (f) the corresponding EDX elemental mapping images of the Ni, Co, and O, respectively.
XPS spectra of urchin-like NiCo2O4 spheres: (a) Full spectrum; (b) Co 2p; (c) Ni 2p; (d) O 1s.

(a) CVs of ORR on NiCo2O4 spheres in N2 and O2-saturated 0.1 M KOH aqueous solution before and after 1000 cycles; (b) Linear sweep voltammetric curves of ORR at NiCo2O4 electrode in an O2-saturated 0.1 M KOH aqueous solution before and after 1000 cycles.

head.ce:title: Facile hydrothermal synthesis of urchin-like NiCo2O4 spheres as efficient electrocatalysts for oxygen reduction reaction

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering/Guangzhou Key Laboratory for Environmentally Functional Materials and Technology, Guangzhou University, No. 230, Waihuan Xi Road, Guangzhou 510006, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering/Guangzhou Key Laboratory for Environmentally Functional Materials and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Guangzhou Institute of Railway Technology, Guangzhou 510430, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this work, we have successfully prepared urchin-like NiCo2O4 spheres via a facile hydrothermal method without any templates or surfactants. The as-prepared urchin-like NiCo2O4 spheres have uniform diameters of 6 μm with numerous small nanorods radially-grown from the center. Typical nanorods have diameters of 20–50 nm and lengths of 2–3 μm. Remarkably, urchin-like NiCo2O4 nanostructure as an alternative non-precious metal electrocatalyst for ORR exhibits catalytic performance and excellent cycling stability in alkaline environment.

ce:section.ce:para: The oxygen reduction reaction (ORR) has been one of the main focuses of electrochemistry in the past decades owing to its wide application in the field of energy storage and conversion [1–3]. Pt metal and Pt-based materials are known to be the most active catalysts for ORR, but the high cost and scarcity of Pt hamper further development of fuel cell and metal–air batteries technologies based on these materials [4–6]. It is of great importance and interest to develop alternative inexpensive catalysts with high activity and wide availability. In this respect, metal oxides [7,8], nitrogen-coordinated metal on carbon [9–11] and metal-free doped carbon materials [12–14] have been actively pursued as ORR catalysts. However, the design and development of a low cost and high activity ORR catalyst is still a tremendous challenge.

Mixed valence oxides of transition metals with a spinel structure are an important class of metal oxides that exhibit ORR catalytic activity in alkaline solution [15]. Due to the activity, low cost, facile synthesis, and high stability, spinel metal oxides have been investigated extensively as electrocatalysts [16,17]. Recently, manganese oxides, cobalt oxides, Mn-based and Co-based composite oxides show high activity and stability as non-precious metal ORR catalysts [18–20]. Among them, the spinel-type composite manganese oxides such as Mn x Cu1−x Co2O4 [16], Co x Mn3−x O4 [21], MnCo2O4 [22–24] and CaMn2O4 [25] have been fabricated and exhibit remarkable electrocatalytic activities toward the ORR in alkaline conditions. However, to the best of our knowledge, the electrochemical properties of urchin-like NiCo2O4 spheres have not been reported for the ORR.

As an interesting binary transition metal oxide, Cobalt–nickel oxide (NiCo2O4) is generally referred to as a spinel structure in which nickel occupied the octahedral sites and cobalt is distributed over both octahedral and tetrahedral sites [26,27]. In recent years, the NiCo2O4 nanostructures have drawn considerable attention due to their unique physical properties and wide variety of applications, most notably their electrocatalytic activity and ferromagnetic properties have been widely investigated [28–30]. In this work, we demonstrate a facile hydrothermal route to prepare urchin-like NiCo2O4 spheres. The formation of high-purity NiCo2O4 spheres allows our investigation of their electrocatalytic activity toward the ORR for the first time.

All the reagents used in the experiment were of analytical grade and were used without further purification. According to the previous report [31], in a typical procedure, 0.01 mol of urea was dissolved in 20 mL deionized water under constant stirring, followed by the addition of 1 mmol Ni(NO3)2·6H2O and 2 mmol Co(NO3)2·6H2O. The mixture was stirred for 30 min, the transparent solution was transferred to a Teflon-lined autoclave (25 mL capacity). The hydrothermal process was conducted at 180 °C for 6 h. The products were collected by filtration, washed with deionized water and ethanol, and finally dried at 60 °C for 6 h. Finally, the dried precipitates were annealed at 400 °C under air for 3 h to obtain the hierarchical porous NiCo2O4 nanosheets.

The surface morphology of the products was characterized by field emission scanning electron microscope (SEM, FEI, Quanta 400). Transmission electron microscope (TEM, JEM-2010HR), high-resolution TEM (HRTEM, 200 kV), and electron diffraction (ED) were also utilized to characterize the microstructures of the products. The obtained products were also analyzed by X-ray diffraction (XRD, Bruker, D8 Advance) to determine the crystal structures. X-ray energy dispersive spectroscopy (EDS), X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, ESCALAB 250) and Laser micro-Raman spectrometry (Renishaw inVia) were applied to study the chemical compositions of the products. Nitrogen adsorption/desorption measurements were conducted on an ASAP 2020M instrument.

The electrochemical measurements were conducted on a PINE instrument in a three-electrode cell by using a reversible hydrogen electrode (RHE) as the reference electrode and a platinum foil as the counter electrode. The working electrode was a glass carbon rotating disk with the diameter of 5 mm. The NiCo2O4 sample (10.0 mg) was added into 1.9 mL ethanol and 0.1 mL Nafion solution (5 wt%, DuPont, USA) and ultrasonicated for 30 min to obtain a well-dispersed ink. The ink was quantitatively transferred onto the surface of the glassy carbon electrode and dried under infrared lamp to obtain an electrocatalyst thin film. The electrocatalyst loading was 1.2 mg cm−2. The electrochemical tests were performed in oxygen saturated 0.1 mol L−1 KOH solution at room temperature at a sweep rate of 5 mV s−1 under the rotating speed of 1600 rpm.

The urchin-like NiCo2O4 spheres were prepared by a simple hydrothermal process. The sample phase was identified by XRD and a typical XRD pattern is shown in Fig. 1 (a). All the diffraction peaks can be readily assigned to the cubic phase of NiCo2O4 (JCPDF card: 20-0781, Space group: F*3(202)) with lattice constants a = b = c = 8.11 Å. The product is highly pure since no peaks of any impurity phase could be observed from this pattern. Furthermore, the intense and sharp peaks indicate that the product is well crystallized. Fig. 1(b) shows a typical low-magnification SEM image of the as-prepared NiCo2O4. It is observed that large numbers of spherical urchin shape structures of NiCo2O4 are obtained with uniform diameters of 6 μm. Fig. 1(c) and (d) shows high-magnification SEM images of the product, clearly revealing each urchin-like NiCo2O4 sphere has uniform diameters of 6 μm with numerous small nanorods radially-grown from the center.

Fig. 2 (a) and (b) shows the TEM image of an individual NiCo2O4 sphere with urchin-like morphology. The urchin-like NiCo2O4 sphere is constructed by a large number of nanorods, and the typical diameter and length of these nanorods are 20–50 nm and 2–3 μm, respectively. The corresponding selected-area electron diffraction (SAED) pattern reveals the nanorods exhibiting single-crystalline nature. Fig. 2(e) is a HRTEM image of the square area from Fig. 2(d). The clear lattice fringes with a d-spacing of 0.29 nm can be well indexed to the (220) plane of cubic NiCo2O4. In order to further elucidate microscopic structure, the TEM-EDX (energy dispersive X-ray) elemental full and line mapping techniques were applied to an individual nanorod. As shown in Fig. 2(f), it can be seen that Co, Ni, and O are distributed as like nanorod. Furthermore, the typical Raman spectrum of the NiCo2O4 sample is also shown in Figure S2. The results further confirm that urchin-like spheres are composed of NiCo2O4.

The more detailed elemental composition and the oxidation state of the as-prepared NiCo2O4 spheres were further characterized by XPS measurements and the corresponding results are presented in Fig. 3 (a)–(d). Fig. 3(a) shows the survey XPS spectrum of urchin-like NiCo2O4 spheres. By using a Gaussian fitting method, the Co 2p emission spectrum (Fig. 3(b)) was best fitted with two spin-orbit doublets, characteristic of Co2+ and Co3+, and one shakeup satellite (identified as “Sat.”) [29]. The Ni 2p was also fitted with two spin-orbit doublets and two shakeup satellites. Two kinds of nickel species have been detected and assigned to species containing Ni2+ and Ni3+. Specially, the fitting peaks at binding energies of 855.3 and 872.9 eV are ascribed to Ni2+, while the peaks at 856.9 and 874.5 eV are ascribed to Ni3+ [32]. The high-resolution spectrum for the O 1s region in Fig. 3(d) shows four oxygen contributions, which have been denoted as O1, O2, O3, and O4. The fitting peak of O1 at 529.6 eV is typical of metal–oxygen bonds [33]. The component O2 sitting at 531 eV is usually associated with oxygen in OH− groups, indicating the presence of the NiCo2O4 material is hydroxylated to some extent as a result of either surface hydroxide or substitution of oxygen atoms at the surface by hydroxyl groups [33,34]. The well-resolved O3 component corresponds to a higher number of defect sites with low oxygen coordination in the material with small particle size [35]. Furthermore, the component O4 can be attributed to multiplicity of physi- and chemisorbed water at or near the surface [36,37]. The XPS data suggests that the surface of the as-prepared NiCo2O4 sample has a composition containing Co2+, Co3+, Ni2+, and Ni3+. Thus, the formula of NiCo2O4 can be generally expressed as follows: Co2+ 1−x Co3+
x [Co3+Ni2+
x Ni3+ 1−x ]O4 (0 < x < 1) (the cations within the square bracket are in octahedral sites and the outside ones occupy the tetrahedral sites) [37,38]. In addition, element analysis by XPS and ICP reveal that the atomic ratio of Co to Ni is close to 2:1, which suggests the Co to Ni ratio of NiCo2O4 is similar to that in the precursor solution. The NiCo2O4 sample was also investigated by nitrogen adsorption and desorption measurement at 77 K. As shown in Figure S1, the specific surface areas of NiCo2O4 samples have been 92.26 m2 g−1. The BET result is in agreement with the TEM observations discussed above, showing urchin-like structure with relatively high surface area. The structural feature gives implications of the applications in electrocatalytic field, which comes as following below.

The electrocatalytic activity of the urchin-like NiCo2O4 spheres toward ORR in 0.1 mol L−1 KOH solution was evaluated by using a conventional three-electrode system. First, the cyclic voltammograms (CVs) were performed in N2 and O2-saturated 0.1 mol L−1 KOH solution at a scan rate of 5 mV s−1 on a RDE with glassy-carbon substrate. As shown in Fig. 4 (a), featureless peak currents with the potential range between 0 and 1.2 V were observed for the urchin-like NiCo2O4 spheres in the N2-saturated solution. In contrast, a well-defined cathodic peak emerges in the reduction current for NiCo2O4 samples when the electrolyte solution was saturated with O2. The corresponding ORR peak potential of the initial cycle is located at 0.61 V, about 100 mV more positive than that of 1000th cycle. The results demonstrate that urchin-like NiCo2O4 spheres have a pronounced electrocatalytic ORR activity. Subsequently, to gain further information about the electrocatalytic ORR process, the reaction kinetics was studied by voltammetry at a rotating-disk electrode. Fig. 4(b) displays the linear sweeping voltammograms (LSVs) of NiCo2O4 spheres at 1600 rpm and a potential sweep rate of 5 mV s−1. It is a meaningful finding that the prepared NiCo2O4 sample shows ORR activity. As is well known the ORR is usually catalyzed by noble metals which causes high cost of fuel cells, the research community has been endeavoring to replace the noble metal catalyst with non-noble metals [39]. The onset potentials for ORR at the urchin-like NiCo2O4 spheres are at ∼0.83 V and 0.36 V, respectively. It demonstrates that the ORR process catalyzed by the urchin-like NiCo2O4 spheres is a two-step two-electron pathway with the formation of intermediate HO2 − ions [34,40]. After 1000 cycles, the half-wave potentials of NiCo2O4 samples only shifted 20 mV in Fig. 4(b), which proves superior cycling stabilities of the NiCo2O4 samples in ORR. The stable catalytic activity of NiCo2O4 spheres may be ascribed to the chemical and structural robustness of spinel in an alkaline environment. Along this line, our further study is underway by investigating the effect on the catalytic activity of shapes. Impressively, the urchin-like NiCo2O4 spheres are promising candidates for building ORR catalysts with superior stability.

In summary, we report a facile and effective approach to the preparation of urchin-like NiCo2O4 spheres on a large scale. The urchin-like NiCo2O4 spheres exhibit electrocatalytic activity and long-term stability toward ORR in alkaline electrolytes. It is expected that the urchin-like NiCo2O4 spheres have potential applications in future fuel cells and other catalytic applications.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by Natural Science Foundations of China (Grant No. 51102054), the Natural Science Foundations of Guangdong Province (Grant No. s2012010009719), and the Innovative Talents Cultivation Project of Guangdong Province (Grant No. LYM11096) and the Science and Technology Project of Guangzhou (Grant No. 12C52011624 and 12C72011619).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data related to this article can be found at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.ijhydene.2013.03.092.
